label: verify-263-cal-app-2d-41
system:
You are the citation verifier in a legal-exploration pipeline. You are given one authority as cited by the pipeline's components, plus snippets showing how it was used. Using web search (prefer primary/official sources: leginfo.legislature.ca.gov, courts.ca.gov, law.justia.com, casetext.com, codes.findlaw.com, fppc.ca.gov), determine:
1. Does the authority exist?
2. Is the citation form correct (reporter cite, year, section number)?
3. Does it plausibly stand for what the snippets use it for?

Statuses: VERIFIED (exists, cite essentially right, usage consistent with what you found); MISCHARACTERIZED (exists but does not support the claimed use, or the cite attaches to the wrong case/section); NOT_FOUND (cannot locate after genuine searching); CONTESTED (real sources point in conflicting directions, or scope is genuinely unsettled); UNCLEAR (could not determine within a reasonable search).

You are NOT determining whether the authority is still good law (no Shepardizing) — say what you actually confirmed, no more. Be strict: a close-but-wrong reporter cite or a holding stretched past what sources show is MISCHARACTERIZED, not VERIFIED.
prompt:
## Authority as cited (variants seen in the documents)

- 263 Cal.App.2d 41

## How the documents used it

[delphi] … 781 (2)

**Cited by only one panelist:**
- Cal. Const. art. I, § 3(b) (Prop 59)
- Gov. Code §§ 54960 and 54960.5 — standing and fee-shifting
- *Sacramento Newspaper Guild v. Sacramento County Bd. of Supervisors* (1968) 263 Cal.App.2d 41
- *Common Cause v. Stirling* (1983) 147 Cal.App.3d 518 (cited tentatively)
- 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 239 (2002) (cited tentatively)
- Legislative history of the 1993–94 amendments to § 54952.2

Nearly all panelists flagged…

[tetlock] … deliberation, not just whether the vote itself was held in open session (substance-over-form; see the foundational 'rolling quorum' rationale in Sacramento Newspaper Guild v. Sacramento County Bd. of Supervisors (1968) 263 Cal.App.2d 41 — verify continued vitality).
- Whether a §54952.2 serial-meeting violation is independently reachable via §54960.1 depends on treating it as a violation of §54953's open-meeting mandate (since §54952.2 defines 'meeting…

[naive_fable] … "Deliberation" is construed very broadly: it includes "the collective acquisition and exchange of facts preliminary to the ultimate decision." *Sacramento Newspaper Guild v. Sacramento County Bd. of Supervisors* (1968) 263 Cal.App.2d 41; *Frazer v. Dixon Unified School Dist.* (1993) 18 Cal.App.4th 781.
- The classic condemned structures are the "daisy chain" (A→B→C) and the "hub and spoke" (staffer or intermediary polls members and relays views). *Stoc…

## Context

The underlying question is a California public-agency hypothetical: q1 — The AI assistant as serial-meeting intermediary (Brown Act).

Verify this authority per your instructions.

canonical_cite.description: the correct full citation, if determinable

note.description: 1-3 sentences: what was confirmed or refuted, and the correct cite

text: {"status":"VERIFIED","canonical_cite":"Sacramento Newspaper Guild v. Sacramento County Bd. of Supervisors (1968) 263 Cal.App.2d 41 (3d Dist., decided June 12, 1968)","note":"Citation is accurate (reporter, page, year, court all confirmed via Justia/FindLaw). The case does hold that \"deliberation\" connotes not just collective discussion but \"the collective acquisition and exchange of facts preliminary to the ultimate decision\" — exactly as used by [naive_fable] for the broad-deliberation point. The [tetlock] snippet's label of it as the \"foundational 'rolling quorum' rationale\" is a slight stretch on the facts (the case itself involved one physical luncheon gathering of the full board, not serial/successive contacts), but this broad-deliberation holding is routinely cited by Brown Act secondary sources (AG Brown Act guide, city-attorney CLE materials) as the doctrinal ancestor of the serial-meeting/rolling-quorum prohibition later codified at §54952.2, and the panelist appropriately flagged \"verify continued vitality.\" Not a misattribution to the wrong case/section.","source_urls":["https://law.justia.com/cases/california/court-of-appeal/2d/263/41.html","https://caselaw.findlaw.com/ca-court-of-appeal/1818917.html","https://oag.ca.gov/system/files/media/the-brown-act.pdf"]}

data.canonical_cite: Sacramento Newspaper Guild v. Sacramento County Bd. of Supervisors (1968) 263 Cal.App.2d 41 (3d Dist., decided June 12, 1968)
data.note: Citation is accurate (reporter, page, year, court all confirmed via Justia/FindLaw). The case does hold that "deliberation" connotes not just collective discussion but "the collective acquisition and exchange of facts preliminary to the ultimate decision" — exactly as used by [naive_fable] for the broad-deliberation point. The [tetlock] snippet's label of it as the "foundational 'rolling quorum' rationale" is a slight stretch on the facts (the case itself involved one physical luncheon gathering of the full board, not serial/successive contacts), but this broad-deliberation holding is routinely cited by Brown Act secondary sources (AG Brown Act guide, city-attorney CLE materials) as the doctrinal ancestor of the serial-meeting/rolling-quorum prohibition later codified at §54952.2, and the panelist appropriately flagged "verify continued vitality." Not a misattribution to the wrong case/section.
